video_title: AI’s Speed Crash: The Structural Bull Market Isn’t Over
gist: Jordi Visser argues the recent 40% 'speed crash' in AI/tech momentum names is a factor unwind and digestion phase inside an intact structural AI bull market, not a systemic or recession event. He frames a new market-structure regime of faster-up/faster-down moves driven by vol-controlled leverage and trading agents, stays convicted on insatiable compute demand and the memory shortage (buying Micron back), and calls crypto (especially Ethereum) the next 12-month beta as tokenization and consumer agents arrive.

themes.theme: AI Speed Crash & New Market Structure

insights.claim: The Morgan Stanley tech momentum factor index fell 40% in 18 days, the worst unwind in that factor since Bloomberg data began in 1998.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Signals a historic momentum-factor deleveraging that resets crowded AI longs and creates lower re-entry prices rather than a fundamental break.
insights.quote: So this is by far by far the worst unwind we have seen in tech mo momentum momentum.

insights.claim: Markets are shifting from the classic 'manias, panics and crashes' cycle to 'bubbles, parabolas and speed crashes' that are faster up, faster down and structurally different, not a business-cycle phenomenon.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Reframes AI drawdowns as a permanent structural regime, so investors should expect recurring violent unwinds and size/leverage accordingly.
insights.quote: Markets are moving from manious panics and crashes... to bubbles, parabas, and speed crashes, faster up, faster down, and structurally different.

insights.claim: Momentum buying is increasingly driven by non-emotional AI agents that chase what is working, and when leverage is stacked on top, vol control kicks in and forces violent unwinds.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Explains why crowded factor trades will unwind faster; watch vol-control and leverage as the mechanism, not fundamentals.
insights.quote: the problem is when you have leverage on top of it, you end up getting this scenario where V control kicks in.

insights.claim: The weekly tape (S&P -1.5%, Nasdaq/QQQ -4%, small caps flat) indicates the selloff is 100% rotation and factor unwind, not a systemic event.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Argues against de-risking broad equity exposure; the pain is concentrated in industrials and tech momentum factors that had gotten stretched.
insights.quote: What this tells me is 100% this is rotation. This is factor unwinds.

insights.claim: This unwind feels like 2007 except spreads are not widening and there is no recession on the horizon, unlike September 2007 when the S&P went negative year-over-year and claims were widening.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Provides a concrete disconfirming checklist (spreads, YoY S&P, claims) to distinguish a factor tremor from a genuine crisis.
insights.quote: It does feel a lot like 07 to me except for one problem. Spreads are not widening and there is no recession on the horizon.

insights.claim: Vol-controlled and systematic funds, pods, and Korean retail will struggle to re-enter AI names because tech-momentum realized vol (techvol ~92) is now roughly seven times S&P vol, forcing smaller equal-risk position sizes.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Implies AI-name rallies will be capped near-term because the marginal leveraged buyer is mechanically sidelined until vol decays.
insights.quote: it's going to be very difficult to get back in because the margins are going to be higher now

insights.claim: Because everything is being tokenized and trading 24 hours a day, single-asset vol spikes now decouple from S&P vol, and this market-structure change is permanent.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Favors long-horizon allocators over day-to-day traders and argues idiosyncratic 100-year vol storms will recur regularly.

insights.claim: Hyperscaler debt-to-equity is next to nothing and their combined RPOs (contractual orders) exceed what they can fill only because they lack compute, so AI-related debt fears are misplaced.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Debunks the 'AI debt bomb' narrative; the debt funds compute against booked demand, so credit risk in the leaders is minimal.
insights.quote: Debt to equity on these companies is next to nothing.

insights.claim: Compute demand will remain insatiable and the world will not have enough compute for the billions of coming agents within the next four years.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Underpins a multi-year bull case for chips, memory and infrastructure regardless of near-term drawdowns.
insights.quote: We will not be there anytime in the next four years where we have enough compute for all the intelligence in the billions of agents that are coming.

insights.claim: Agents consume tokens, not physical goods, and token consumption grows exponentially as models get smarter, so the compute demand curve keeps rising and enterprise agents are only the first wave before consumer agents.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Gives a demand mental model for why infrastructure spend does not plateau; watch token-consumption growth as the key indicator.
insights.quote: They don't get paid. They get paid in tokens. And the amount of tokens they need will grow as they continue to get smarter.

insights.claim: The leading US model companies (excluding OpenAI and Anthropic) will be protected by the government because of the AI race against China.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Suggests policy backstop reduces tail risk for AI leaders, supporting the buy-the-dip stance.
insights.quote: they're going to be protected by the government. So, I really don't know why you guys are getting all excited about the the leading model companies in the US when we're race against China.

insights.claim: Open source will eventually dominate most enterprises but not near-term; AI-native companies will win while incumbent public companies lose, and by 2030 few physical companies have a guaranteed three-year survival horizon.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Implies a long-term short thesis on disrupted incumbents and a premium on AI-native business models.
insights.quote: AI native companies will win. Public companies will lose.

insights.claim: Jordi Visser is re-buying Micron after an 8-to-10-bagger exit in early June, including buying back at higher levels than his average sale price, because new contracts and dramatically raised earnings estimates changed the picture.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A specific actionable re-entry into memory on the correction; signals conviction that the DRAM cycle has more room.
insights.quote: I even bought Micron at higher levels than the average price of where I got out.

insights.claim: There is a memory shortage with DRAM prices for DDR3, DDR4 and DDR5 all rising, and memory makers are signing long-term contracts, supporting the thesis that 'AI equals memory.'
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Frames memory as a supply-constrained pure-play on AI; watch DRAM pricing and whether memory acts badly on good news for a bottom signal.
insights.quote: AI equals memory. And I'm going to keep saying that AI equals memory.

insights.claim: Micron's biggest first-hour trading volume since its ~$1300-index peak, plus reclaiming the VWAP early, marks a potential trend change and a reason to buy on the tape.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Offers a tape-reading entry signal; volume expansion off lows can precede a bottom in a beaten-down leader.
insights.quote: it was the biggest volume first hour way above the average since the peak since we were at 1300.

insights.claim: Micron mirrors Nvidia's 2023-24 head-and-shoulders pattern, and buying the reclaim of a broken neckline in a structural bull market is one of Visser's favorite setups.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Gives a chart-pattern trigger (neckline reclaim) to time an add in Micron; analogizes to Nvidia/Amazon structural-bull breakouts.
insights.quote: One of my favorite charts to buy is a structural bull market when you break a neckline of a head and shoulders and then you immediately go back up.

insights.claim: Even with excellent earnings, Nvidia's reactions have turned negative since its June peak beat, so violent post-earnings upside in memory names should not be assumed.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Warns that great numbers no longer guarantee upside once a name has peaked; watch price reaction to earnings as the tell.
insights.quote: So if you just think Micron's going to go up again in violent fashion even after the earnings are great I don't see it happening.

insights.claim: Software AI names like Adobe and Salesforce have downward-sloping 200-day moving averages and are 'toast' unless they pivot their business, while ~85% of Visser's portfolio still has upward 200-days.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Separates disruptable software from still-uptrending AI infrastructure; a relative-value short/avoid signal on legacy SaaS.
insights.quote: These are downward moving 200 day moving averages. These stocks are toast.

insights.claim: 43 names into S&P 500 earnings, the sales surprise is 4% and the earnings surprise 16%, with JP Morgan (+34% surprise, stock +10%) and Goldman Sachs (+45% beat) both hitting all-time highs.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Strong broad earnings argue GDP is stronger than data suggests and cap downside for the broader market despite the factor unwind.
insights.quote: The sales surprise 4%. Earning surprise 16%.

insights.claim: Bank leaders describe AI as a new structural engine funding years of financing, M&A and capital formation, with the AI infrastructure buildout still in its early stages.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Bank commentary supports a multi-year AI capex and financing cycle, bullish for capital-markets and infrastructure exposure.
insights.quote: The AI infrastructure and investment cycle is still early enough to support years of financing, M&A, and capital formation with inevitable bumps along the way.

insights.claim: The best recession signal is the S&P 500 turning negative year-over-year, and markets are nowhere near that, so 'good as it gets' commentary from Jamie Dimon is not a top signal.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Offers a concrete top/recession indicator to monitor; until S&P YoY goes negative, bearish macro calls are premature.
insights.quote: the best recession signal... the S&P year-over-year turns negative. We're not even in the ballpark of that happening anytime soon.

insights.claim: Core PCE and CPI came in much lower than expected, pushing the Atlanta Fed sticky-core 12-month and Truflation to new lows, undercutting economists calling for rate hikes.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Disinflation supports the case for cuts/easier policy despite hawkish rhetoric; positions for lower-rate beneficiaries.
insights.quote: You have true flation at new lows. You have core CPI turned down.

insights.claim: Kevin Warsh is rhetorically hawkish but intellectually reformist, and his stance is about Fed reform and forward-looking data, not genuine hawkishness; markets are misreading him.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: If Warsh is dovish/reformist in substance, positioning for hawkish policy is a mistake; a rate-cut and reform tailwind is likely.
insights.quote: Worsh is rhetorically hawkish about the inflation outcome, but intellectually reformist

insights.claim: Warsh argues AI will raise prices but it should not be called inflation, implying central banks should weight forward-looking data over backward-looking prints.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A reframing that could justify easier policy through an AI-driven price shift; watch for this narrative to shape Fed communication.
insights.quote: he talks about AI will raise prices but don't call it inflation.

insights.claim: Demis Hassabis, whom Visser rates as the most honest and credible AGI voice, writes that AGI exhibiting all the brain's cognitive capabilities is probably only a few short years away.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A near-term AGI timeline from a top source justifies weighting forward-looking AI disruption heavily in allocation decisions.
insights.quote: The artificial general intelligence, a system that exhibits all the cognitive capabilities the brain has, is probably only a few short years away.

insights.claim: Hassabis first predicted around 2010 that AGI would arrive around 2030, and frames the current moment as the foothills of the singularity comparable to the discovery of fire or electricity.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A consistent 2030 AGI call from a credible source frames the investment horizon; accelerated breakthroughs in drug discovery, energy and materials become investable themes.
insights.quote: think Kevin Worsh, I think we will realize we were standing in the foothills of the singularity

insights.claim: Visser expects crypto, not a re-entry into AI infrastructure, to be where the beta is over the next 12 months, and rotated a good portion of AI-sale proceeds into Bitcoin, Ethereum and silver.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: An actionable rotation call away from AI infrastructure and toward crypto as the next high-beta trade for institutional money.
insights.quote: this is going to be where the beta is over the next 12 months. Uh it's not going to be joining back into the infrastructure trade.

insights.claim: Ethereum is the crypto to watch and is having its best month since last August, up 17% month-to-date, and should outperform Bitcoin if an agent revolution is underway.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Directs relative-value attention to ETH/BTC; a break above the 200-day on the ratio would confirm the agent-driven thesis.
insights.quote: Ethereum is having its best month, up 17% monthto date since August of last year.

insights.claim: The rise of consumer agents will drive crypto adoption, with Bitcoin positioned as the collateral of the future because it has a moat built on scarcity and cannot be disrupted by AI.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Frames Bitcoin as the purest un-disruptable AI trade; positions crypto as agent-economy infrastructure rather than a speculative sideshow.
insights.quote: Bitcoin is the collateral of the future.

insights.claim: Stripe put in a bid for PayPal, a stablecoin story following Stripe's 2024 $1.1B acquisition of Bridge, and PayPal is in Visser's 40-name crypto thematic index.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: M&A signal that stablecoin infrastructure is consolidating; PayPal and payment/stablecoin names are catalysts to watch.
insights.quote: Stripe put in a bid for PayPal.

insights.claim: Institutional tokenization is accelerating, with JP Morgan, BlackRock and Goldman tokenizing stocks and treasuries and bank crypto adoption hitting a 32% average.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Major institutions building tokenized real-world-asset rails validates the tokenization theme as an early-stage growth ecosystem.
insights.quote: JP Morgan, Black Rockck, and Goldman tokenized stocks and treasuries. That was this week.

insights.claim: Regulatory momentum is building for crypto, with Japan reclassifying Bitcoin as a financial asset, South Korea writing crypto into its state asset rulebook and moving to allow spot Bitcoin ETFs, while the US Clarity Act sits at a ~40% passage chance.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Global regulatory legitimization is a structural tailwind for crypto flows even if US legislation stalls.
insights.quote: Japan's landmark vote reclassifies Bitcoin and crypto as financial assets.

insights.claim: A break above the 200-day moving average in Bitcoin, which Visser sees as a Q4 story around October-November, would trigger heavy institutional crypto involvement over the following 12 months.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Gives a specific technical trigger and timeframe for the crypto beta thesis to activate; a level to watch into Q4.
insights.quote: if you break the 200 day moving average, I believe most people, if not all, will be heavily involved in crypto over the next 12 months.
